coredata.dc:title: Leaf-derived sulfonated carbon dots: efficient and recoverable catalysts to synthesize 5-hydroxymethylfurfural from fructose
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Y.
Li, M.
Gao, Z.
Wang, L.
Zhang, J.
coredata.dc:description: Sulfonated carbon dots (SCDs) were synthesized from plant leaves via continuously hydrothermal treatment by hydrogen peroxide and sulfuric acid, used as catalyst for converting fructose to 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF). Owing to nanosize effect and moderate acidic intensity, SCDs could thoroughly distribute in the solvent with an improved interfacial compatibility and selectively convert fructose to HMF. Under the optimal condition, the yield of HMF was 92.6% along with a fructose conversion of 100%, benefiting from a low activation energy of 52.9 kJ/mol when dimethylsulfoxide was used as solvent. The SCDs catalyst can be recovered, after six recycles, the fructose conversion and HMF yield were remained 66.1% and 56.2% under condition with incompletely conversion of fructose, respectively. This work provides a sustainable route to prepare carbon dots with a superior catalytic performance for converting biomass to important biobased platform chemicals.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon material
Solid acids
Selectivity
Dehydration
Recyclability

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Physicochemical properties of CDs and SCDs
Analysis of reaction kinetics and activation energy
Effect of solvent and catalyst on fructose conversion

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ningbo Municipal Bureau of Science and Technology
Fujian Institute of Innovation, Chinese Academy of Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from Chinese Academy of Sciences ( QYZDB-SSW-JSC037 ), Ningbo Science and Technology Bureau ( 2018B10056 , 2019B10096 ), and Fujian Institute of Innovation ( FJCXY18020202 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A synthesis diagram of SCDs derived from plant leaves. SCDs, sulfonated carbon dots.

TEM images of (a) CDs and (b) SCDs. Upper right insets in (a) and (b): high-resolution TEM images of CDs and SCDs. Lower right insets in (a) and (b): size distributions of CDs and SCDs. (c) XRD patterns and (d) Raman spectra of CDs (gray line) and SCDs (black line). SCDs, sulfonated carbon dots; TEM, transmission electron microscopy; XRD. X-ray diffraction.

(a) XPS survey spectra of CDs and SCDs, (b) C1s, and (c) S2p spectra of SCDs, (d) N1s spectrum of the SCDs, (e) FTIR spectra of CDs and SCDs, (f) NH3-TPD profile of the SCDs. SCDs, sulfonated carbon dots; XPS, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy; FTIR, Fourier-transform infrared; NH3-TPD, temperature-programmed desorption of ammonia.

Effects of time and temperature on (a) fructose conversion and (b) HMF yield of the catalytic reaction. (c) Effect of catalyst dosage on fructose conversion and HMF yield. HMF, 5-hydroxymethylfurfural.

(a) Plots of -ln(1-Conversion fructose) versus time at different temperatures; (b) Plot of (ln k) versus (1/T × 103) for the dehydration of fructose (Reaction conditions: 1.0 mL DMSO, 20 mg fructose, 8 mg SCDs catalyst). SCDs, sulfonated carbon dots.
Effect of reaction solvent on fructose conversion and HMF yield (1.0 mL solvents, 20 mg fructose, 8 mg SCDs, 110 °C, 60 min). HMF, 5-hydroxymethylfurfural.

Catalytic performances of SCDs derived from various plant leaves for converting fructose to HMF (1.0 mL DMSO, 20 mg fructose, 8 mg SCDs, 110 °C, 60 min). SCDs, sulfonated carbon dots; HMF, 5-hydroxymethylfurfural; DMSO, dimethylsulfoxide.
Recycles of SCDs catalyst (1.0 mL DMSO, 20 mg fructose, 8 mg SCDs, 110 °C, 20 min). SCDs, sulfonated carbon dots; DMSO, dimethylsulfoxide.
Effects of different catalysts and solvents on fructose conversion to HMF.

head.ce:title: Leaf-derived sulfonated carbon dots: efficient and recoverable catalysts to synthesize 5-hydroxymethylfurfural from fructose

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Polymeric Materials Technology and Application of Zhejiang Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Polymeric Materials Technology and Application of Zhejiang Province, Ningbo 315201, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sulfonated carbon dots (SCDs) were synthesized from plant leaves via continuously hydrothermal treatment by hydrogen peroxide and sulfuric acid, used as catalyst for converting fructose to 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF). Owing to nanosize effect and moderate acidic intensity, SCDs could thoroughly distribute in the solvent with an improved interfacial compatibility and selectively convert fructose to HMF. Under the optimal condition, the yield of HMF was 92.6% along with a fructose conversion of 100%, benefiting from a low activation energy of 52.9 kJ/mol when dimethylsulfoxide was used as solvent. The SCDs catalyst can be recovered, after six recycles, the fructose conversion and HMF yield were remained 66.1% and 56.2% under condition with incompletely conversion of fructose, respectively. This work provides a sustainable route to prepare carbon dots with a superior catalytic performance for converting biomass to important biobased platform chemicals.

ce:section.ce:para: Both rapid consumption of petrochemicals and depletion of fossil fuels raise the concern on the development of sustainable energy and renewable feedstock. The catalytic conversion of carbohydrate biomass into highly valuable chemicals, in particular, the acid-catalyzed dehydration of fructose to 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) as a versatile and key platform compound with important applications in clean fuels, biodegradable polymer, and solvents, has attracted tremendous attentions [1–4].

During the past two decades, there has been an emergence of numerous researches on the fructose-to-HMF process by many kinds of catalysts such as liquid acids [5,6], transition metal ions [7], ionic liquid [8], strong acid cation exchange resins [9], sulfonated carbonaceous materials [10], and other solid acids [11]. Liquid acids are cheap and effective, but they present difficult recycling and serious corrosion problems. Transition metal ions, especially metal chloride catalysts, are toxic and environmentally unfriendly. Acidic zeolites offer easy separation but usually suffer from pore blocking by the humins and its precursors, giving burden on optimizing the pore size distribution [12]. Strong acidic cation exchange resins are mainly limited by their low thermal stability and insufficient surface area [13]. Differently, sulfonated carbonaceous materials have become a rising star in the family of solid acids owing to their low-cost synthesis, facile surface functionalization, tunable acid strength, and relative chemical inertness [14,15]. Qi et al. [16] prepared cellulose-derived amorphous carbon and achieved an HMF yield of 83%. Yang et al. used a hydrothermal and sulfonation method to synthesize sulfonated carbon microspheres, over which a HMF yield of 90% was achieved in dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) solvent at 160 °C after 1.5 h [17]. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) with lateral sizes below 10 nm represent a fascinating class of nanocarbons, showing multiple applications in bioimaging, drug delivery, sensors, optoelectronics, and photocatalysis [18–20] because of their outstanding optical properties, low environmental hazard, excellent biocompatibility, and high chemical stability [18,21,22]. Current studies focus on the photoluminescence nature of CDs while less attention has been paid to their function to host a variety of catalytically active surface groups. Although the sulfonated graphene quantum dots could selective conversion of carbohydrates to HMF, the low yield of HMF and recovery ability limited their further applications [23].

Herein, a novel quasi-homogeneous catalyst for conversion fructose to HMF was investigated. The sulfonated carbon dots (SCDs) as a solid acid catalyst were synthesized by using plant leaves as a precursor via hydrogen peroxide pretreatment and sulfuric acid treatment. The physicochemical properties of SCDs were determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, and temperature-programmed desorption of ammonia (NH3-TPD). To obtain the high yield of HMF, the optimal conditions for fructose conversion were investigated. Furthermore, the recyclability of catalyst was also performed to evaluate its catalytic ability.

Various plant leaves were picked from the campus of Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology & Engineering, thoroughly washed by water and dried in air. Fructose was provided by Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt%), sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98 wt%), DMSO, sulfolane, dimethylacetamide (DMA), dimethylformamide (DMF), tetrahydrofuran (THF), N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), and ethyl acetate (EtOAc) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Deionized water was used throughout the experiments.

Plant leaves were pretreated by H2O2 to fabricate carbon dots (CDs) as shown in Fig. 1 . In a typical procedure, 1.0 g camphor leaves were put into a 50-mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave containing 30 mL H2O2 (4 wt%) solution. The autoclave was sealed and heated in an electric oven at 180 °C for 12 h. After the autoclave cooled down naturally to room temperature, the resulting solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to remove the residue, the centrifugal liquor was dialyzed in a dialysis tube (molecular weight cutoff: 1000 Da) for three days (changed deionized water every 8 h) to obtain the CDs solution. The CDs powder was obtained by freeze-drying treatment of CDs solution.

SCDs were prepared by sulfonation of CDs with concentrated H2SO4 (Fig. 1). For a typical synthesis, 0.1 g CDs were added into 2 mL concentrated H2SO4 (98%), and then heated at 140 °C for 6 h. After the reaction was completed, 28 mL deionized water was slowly added into the reacted solution to dilute. Then, the liquor was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to remove the residue solid, the centrifugal liquor was dialyzed in a dialysis tube (molecular weight cutoff: 1000 Da) for three days (changed deionized water every 8 h) to obtain the SCDs solution. The SCDs powder was obtained by freeze-drying treatment of SCDs solution.

TEM images were taken on a JEM-2100 electron microscope operating at an accelerated voltage of 200 kV [24]. XRD patterns were recorded on a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer with Cu Ká radiation (1.54056 Å). Raman spectra of the as-prepared CDs samples were obtained on a Renishaw in Via-reflex spectroscopy with a laser at an excitation wavelength of 325 nm, owing to the strong fluorescence interference excited under other wavelengths [25], but the excitation wavelength of SCDs was 532 nm. XPS tests were performed with an AXIS UL TRA DLD spectrograph with Al/Kα as the source. Element analysis was determined on an Elementar analyzer with a thermal conductivity detector. FTIR spectra were collected in the KBr medium on a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer. NH3-TPD was carried out on an apparatus AUTOCHEM II 2920. The catalyst (50 mg) was put into the quartz reactor, and then the temperature was raised to 100 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min in a He flow of 45 mL/min, and the residence time was 1 h. Then, the temperature was decreased to 90 °C and then a flow of 10% NH3/He (20 mL/min) was pulsed into the reactor until the acid sites were saturated with NH3. After the adsorbed NH3 was removed by He and the baseline was stable, the temperature was increased from 90 °C to 550 °C with a ramp of 10 °C/min. The acid density of SCDs was quantified by neutralization titration based on the reported procedures [26,27]. The densities of SO3H groups were thus estimated based on the sulfur content determined from sample compositions obtained by elemental analysis. The total SO3H + COOH and SO3H + COOH + OH contents were estimated from the exchange of Na+ in aqueous NaCl and NaOH solutions, respectively, to afford the proportions of each functional group [28–30].

Briefly, 20 mg fructose was dissolved into 1 mL DMSO, and then the designed amount of SCDs were added as the catalyst. Finally, the reactor was put into an oil bath for controlling the reaction temperature (60–110 °C) and residence time (0–100 min) at a rate of 500 r/min. When the reaction reached completion, the reaction mixture was diluted with deionized water for the product analysis by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). The fructose was detected on an Agilent 1260 HPLC system equipped with an Aminex HPX-87H column (300 × 7.8 mm, Bio-Rad, Hercules, CA, U.S.A.) and a differential refractive index detector [31]. The mobile phase was 5 mM H2SO4, the injection volume, flow rate, and the column temperature were 10 μL, 0.6 mL/min, and 50 °C, respectively. Quantitative analysis of HMF was performed by HPLC equipped with a ZORBAX SB-C18 column and a UVW detector (278 nm). Water-methanol (95:5 (v/v)) solution was used as the mobile phase. The injection volume, flow rate and the column temperature were 5 μL, 0.8 mL/min and 40 °C, respectively. The standard curve of HMF was performed at five different HMF concentration (0.1, 0.4, 0.8, 1.2, 1.7 mM), the fitting coefficient of R2 was 0.999, each sample was detected twice. Fructose conversion, HMF yield and selectivity were defined as follows:
Fructose conversion
( % )
=
molars of fructose reacted molars of initial fructose
× 100 %
Yield
( % )
=
molars of HMF produced molars of initial fructose
× 100 %
Selectivity
( % )
=
molars of HMF produced molars of fructose reacted
× 100 %

When the reaction reached completion, 1 mL EtOAc was added into the reaction system, and the deposited SCDs were recovered by filtration and repeatedly washed 3 times by DMSO-EtOAc (1:1, v/v) cosolvent. Then, the SCDs was freeze-dried for 24 h. The recovered SCDs was used for the next run under the same conditions. The SCDs were recycled for six times to evaluate the catalyst recovery in the fructose conversion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical properties of CDs and SCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs and SCDs were analyzed by TEM as shown in Fig. 2 a and b. The spherical CDs and SCDs were uniformly dispersion due to the nanoscale diameter of CDs and SCDs were 2.0–5.0 and 1.5–4.5 nm, respectively. The microstructure of CDs absented any lattice fringe, whereas SCDs presented clear lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphite was observed [32]. In the XRD patterns (Fig. 2c), the diffraction peak of the material shifted from 20.5° to 25.9° after sulfuric acid treatment, being close to graphite (002) plane at 26.5°, indicating the sulfuric acid treatment induced further carbonization of CDs and enhanced the graphitization degree of the resultant SCDs. The Raman spectrum of CDs and SCDs (Fig. 2d) exhibited a disordered D band at 1,387 cm−1 and a crystalline G band at 1,581 cm−1, which was suggestive of the coexistence of sp 3 and sp 2 carbon clusters inside the no sulfonated and sulfonated dots [33].

XPS spectra were collected for the analysis of the elemental composition at the surface and chemical state of each element. The survey spectrum of CDs in Fig. 3 a comprised sharp C1s, O1s, and weak N1s signals. The C1s spectrum of SCDs in Fig. 3b could be resolved into five carbon species at 284.5, 285.3, 286.5, 288.0, and 289.0 eV, being associated with C–C/CC, C–N, C–O, CO, and O–CO groups, respectively [34,35]. These adsorption peaks were observed in the XPS spectrum of SCDs, implied that the SCDs contained oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, or sulfuric acid, etc. The additional signals of S2s and S2p of SCDs were observed (Fig. 3a), and the content of sulfur element was 1.51%. Moreover, the deconvolution of the S2p spectrum in Fig. 3c reported two sulfur components at 168.3 and 169.5 eV, being assigned to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 positions of Brønsted acidic SO3H groups [36]. The presence of the peak corresponding to S2p for SCDs confirmed its successful sulfonation in comparison to CDs. The N1s species in SCDs (Fig. 3d) were pyridinic N (398.5 eV), pyrrolic N (399.5 eV), graphitic N (400.6 eV), and pyridinic N-oxide (402.1 eV) [34,35], which originated from the naturally contained nitrogen element in leaves such as protein or amino acids. The elements analysis of CDs and SCDs also confirmed the increase of sulfur element in SCDs after sulfonation of CDs as shown in (Table S1). The FTIR of CDs and SCDs displayed four major absorption bands including the O–H, CO, CC, and C–H, which peaked at 3,403 cm−1, 1,706 cm−1, 1,616 cm−1, and 1,405 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 3e). Furthermore, another two distinctive bands of OSO at 1,178 cm−1 and SO3− at 1031 cm−1 confirmed the actual grafting of SO3H groups on the surface of SCDs [37], being consistent with the XPS analysis. The acidic density was an important property of SCDs as catalyst for converting fructose to HMF. The NH3-TPD profile of SCDs in Fig. 3f showed a broad and intensive peak (100–500 °C) that was usually ascribed to the weak and moderate acid sites [38]. The moderate acid intensity of SCDs possibly benefit to convert fructose to HMF according to the previous report [4,12]. The total acid density was 1.16 mmol/g.

The effects of reaction time and temperature on the fructose conversion and HMF yield were investigated as shown in Fig. 4 a and b. The fructose conversion and HMF yield increased with increasing reaction time and temperature. For example, the fructose conversion increased from 40.9% to 100%, the yield of HMF increased from 34.6% to 92.6%, when the reaction time increased from 10 to 60 min. Furthermore, the yield of HMF changed little when further extended the reaction time at temperature of 110 °C. The increasing temperature speeded up fructose conversion and HMF formation to reach a high value. As seen in Fig. 3a and b, at 80 °C, the fructose conversion and the yield of HMF reached 72.0% and 52.6%, respectively, in 90 min. However, at 110 °C, it only required 20 min to obtain the comparable results. This evidence could be related with the elevating temperature increased the conversion rate and reaction activation energy of fructose to HMF.

The effect of the catalyst dosage on the conversion of fructose to HMF was shown in Fig. 4c. The HMF yield and fructose conversion were 0.79% and 31.2% with the absence of the SCDs catalyst. Interestingly, a tiny amount of SCDs (1 mg) was able to trigger an HMF yield of 77.7% and a fructose conversion of 100%, confirming the prevailing catalytic role of acidic functional groups in the reaction turnover. The yield of HMF reached the maximum of 92.6% when the amount of SCDs was 8 mg. Moreover, the further dehydration of HMF formed the levulinic acid and formic acid, which were detected as 1.3% and 1.1%, respectively, indicating the continuous conversion of HMF already happened at this stage. Further increasing the catalyst dosage resulted in a slight decline of HMF yield, owing to a possibility that the excess strong acid sites may favor the side reactions such as levulinic acid, formic acid, and humins formation [26,39]. The fructose conversion changed with increasing reaction time at different catalyst amount as shown in Figure S1, the kinetic model was shown in (Table S2), indicating the reaction rate increased with increase of catalyst amount. Mass-normalized rates of fructose conversion with different catalyst amount was shown in Table S3, the mass-normalized rates decreased with increase of catalyst amount, indicated that the activity of catalyst decreased when the catalyst amount increased in reaction system. Thus, the optimum conditions for converting fructose to HMF in the presence of SCDs were as follows reaction temperature: 110 °C, residence time: 60 min, catalyst dosage: 8 mg.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of reaction kinetics and activation energy
ce:section.ce:para: The reaction kinetics was investigated at the temperature ranged from 60 to 100 °C. As illustrated in Fig. 5 a, a linear relationship between -ln(1-Conversionfructose) and time was observed after data fitting, validating the first-order model for fructose dehydration step [38,40]. The results also indicated that the reaction rate increased with the increasing reaction temperature, which possibly correlated to the lower activation energy. Fig. 5b showed that the plot of (ln k) versus (1/T × 103) according to the Arrhenius equation reported an activation energy of 52.9 kJ/mol for converting fructose to HMF with SCDs as catalyst, which was much lower compared with the sulfuric acid (99 kJ/mol), ion-exchange resin (103.4 kJ/mol) [9], and mesoporous silica nanoparticles (80.05 kJ/mol) [41]. A superior catalytic activity of SCDs with a low energy barrier to facilitate fructose conversion was therefore demonstrated. This possibly due to the nanoscale effect of SCDs increases the contact between fructose and the active site, which results in the decrease of the reaction energy barrier and the increase of catalytic efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of solvent and catalyst on fructose conversion
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of solvent on the catalytic performance of SCDs was evaluated by using a variety of common solvents (Fig. 6 ). Except for water and sulfolane, the solvents of DMA, DMF, THF, and NMP brought about high fructose conversion but low HMF selectivity, due to form the major byproduct of humins in the reaction process. Clearly, DMSO was a preferred solvent for fructose-to-HMF transformation because it favored the furanoid form of fructose that was easily dehydrated into HMF, and it was reported to stabilize HMF and suppress the side reactions [10]. Moreover, H+ derived from Brønsted acid preferring to interact with DMSO other than fructose, forming [DMSOH]+ as the catalytically active species, the catalytic role of [DMSOH]+ originates from the valence unsaturation of both S and O atoms, as well as the H-mediated effect of –SOH group [42]. The second water molecule release step from fructose was determined by [DMSOH]+ rather than DMSO. The dehydration of fructose in DMSO with presence of sulfuric acid was analyzed by in situ NMR combined with isotopic-labeling, determined the irreversibility of the three steps from the fructofuranosyl oxocarbenium ions to HMF and the analogous pyranose route [43]. Furthermore, the fructose maybe can directly dehydrate water (−3H2O) to produce 5-HMF in DMSO/Brønsted acid reaction system [42].

It was believed that the direct separation of SCDs from DMSO was quite difficult due to their excellent dispersibility in high-polar solvents. Almost all SCDs precipitated after a certain amount of EtOAc was added into DMSO, and then the deposited SCDs could be recycled by filtration. Complete conversion of fructose should be avoided to effectively evaluate the recovery performance of the catalyst [46]. Therefore, the recovery property of SCDs catalyst was conducted at 110 °C for 20 min, and the fructose conversion and HMF yield were 68.7% and 59.9%, respectively. The fructose conversion and HMF yield were remained 66.1% and 56.2%, respectively, even six recycles of the SCDs catalysts (Fig. 8 ). The elements analysis (Table S1) and XPS (Fig. S2) of SCDs and R-SCDs indicated a slight decrease of sulfur in the R-SCDs, which may be caused by the loss of sulfonic acid group after reused, ultimately resulting in a slight decrease in HMF yield, demonstrating a promising future of SCDs as recoverable catalysts for HMF production.

In summary, SCDs with SO3H, COOH, and OH functional groups were synthesized via oxidation and sulfonation of various plant leaves. Such nanomaterials were well distributed in polar solvents and presented appropriate acid strength, that allowed their application as a solid acid for catalytic dehydration of fructose to HMF. When SCDs and DMSO were used as catalyst and solvent, respectively, fructose was selectively converted to HMF, with yield as high as 92.6%. The SCDs catalyst was easily recycled from the reaction solvent, after six recycles, the fructose conversion and the HMF yield were remained 66.1% and 56.2% under condition with incompletely conversion of fructose, respectively. Our research highlights the application of the active and reusable SCDs for catalytic transformation of renewable carbohydrates into new biobased chemicals.

Yaoping Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Resources, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing-original draft. Zhijin Gao: Validation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Lei Wang: Writing-review & editing, Supervision. Mingfu Li: Formal analysis, Supervision, Writing-review & editing. Jian Zhang: Writing-review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known conflict of interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this article.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from Chinese Academy of Sciences (QYZDB-SSW-JSC037), Ningbo Science and Technology Bureau (2018B10056, 2019B10096), and Fujian Institute of Innovation (FJCXY18020202).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtchem.2021.100423.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: 5-Hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) as a building block platform: biological properties, synthesis and synthetic applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.A. Rosatella, S.P. Simeonov, R.F.M. Frade, C.A.M. Afonso, 5-Hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) as a building block platform: Biological properties, synthesis and synthetic applications, Green Chem. 13 (2011) 754-793.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A versatile platform chemical made from renewable Resources

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. van Putten, J.C. van der Waal, E. de Jong, C.B. Rasrendra, H.J. Heeres, J.G. de Vries, Hydroxymethylfurfural, A Versatile Platform Chemical Made from Renewable Resources, Chem Rev. 113 (2013) 1499-1597.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydroxymethylfurfural production from bioresources: past, present and future

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.P. Teong, G. Yi, Y. Zhang, Hydroxymethylfurfural production from bioresources: past, present and future, Green Chem. 16 (2014) 2015-2226.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Catalytic strategy for conversion of fructose to organic dyes, polymers, and liquid fuels

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Chang, I. Bajaj, G.W. Huber, C.T. Maravelias, J.A. Dumesic, Catalytic strategy for conversion of fructose to organic dyes, polymers, and liquid fuels, Green Chem. 22 (2020) 5285-5295.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Kinetics of the decomposition of fructose catalyzed by hydrochloric acid in subcritical water: formation of 5-hydroxymethylfurfural, levulinic, and formic acids

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F.S. Asghari, H. Yoshida, Kinetics of the Decomposition of Fructose Catalyzed by Hydrochloric Acid in Subcritical Water: Formation of 5-Hydroxymethylfurfural, Levulinic, and Formic Acids, Ind. Eng. Chem. Res. 46 (2007) 7703-7710.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A combined experimental and computational study of the mechanism of fructose dehydration to 5-hydroxymethylfurfural in dimethylsulfoxide using Amberlyst 70, PO4 3-/niobic acid, or sulfuric acid catalysts

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zhang, A. Das, R.S. Assary, L.A. Curtiss, E. Weitz, A combined experimental and computational study of the mechanism of fructose dehydration to 5-hydroxymethylfurfural in dimethylsulfoxide using Amberlyst 70, PO43-/niobic acid, or sulfuric acid catalysts, Applied Catalysis B: Environmental 18 (2016) 874-887.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal chlorides in ionic liquid solvents convert sugars to 5-hydroxymethylfurfural

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Zhao, J.E. Holladay, H. Brown, Z.C. Zhang, Metal Chlorides in Ionic Liquid Solvents Convert Sugars to 5-Hydroxymethylfurfural, Science 316 (2007) 1597-1599.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Catalytic conversion of carbohydrates into 5-hydroxymethylfurfural by germanium (IV) chloride in ionic liquids

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Zhang, Q. Wang, H. Xie, W. Liu, Z.K. Zhao, Catalytic Conversion of Carbohydrates into 5-Hydroxymethylfurfural by Germanium (IV) Chloride in Ionic Liquids, ChemSuSChem 4 (2011) 131-138.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Catalytic dehydration of fructose into 5-hydroxymethylfurfural by ion-exchange resin in mixed-aqueous system by microwave heating

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Qi, M. Watanabe, T.M. Aida, J.R.L. Smith, Catalytic dehydration of fructose into 5-hydroxymethylfurfural by ion-exchange resin in mixed-aqueous system by microwave heating, Green Chem. 10 (2008) 799-805.
